champion.narrative: No champion identified. Claudio Díaz (Jefe de Proyectos ERP) is the sole buyer-side OpportunityContactRole and was present on the 22 April demo, but he has not responded to three rep follow-ups (12 May, 26 May, 16 June) over 65 days—passive, not advocating. Gustavo Schlack and Erica Bahamonde (both @onnetfibra.com) attended the demo but have no follow-on engagement recorded and are not in OpportunityContactRoles. EY is driving the process (5 EY consultants on the call), but none have emerged as a Zycus advocate: no EY consultant has shared competitive intelligence, coached Zycus on the shortlist, or pushed the evaluation forward on Zycus's behalf. The relationship is transactional and consultant-led, not champion-driven. Without a champion, Zycus has no internal coaching, no visibility into the RFP scoring or shortlist, no advocate to counter competitors, and no one pushing urgency. The 65-day silence confirms this: a champion would have replied to at least one follow-up or shared the next milestone. The move is to (a) re-engage Claudio directly (not just via EY) to understand his role and influence, (b) ask for an introduction to the procurement director or the comité lead (the real decision influencer), and (c) assess whether one of the EY consultants (Clementina Villalba, Oscar Otero) can be developed into a coach by offering them deal-shaping value (e.g. Zycus implementation best practices, ROI modeling support).

competition.narrative: Competition is unknown. The RFI/RFP process (RFI submitted 28 April per Next Step; RFP due 27 April per 14 April Next Step entry) definitively indicates a multi-vendor evaluation, but On Net Fibra and EY have not shared the shortlist. No competitor was named on the 22 April demo call (transcript and notes show no rival mentions). The Salesforce Competitors__c field is empty. No completed Tasks or Next Step entries reference a specific rival (Coupa, SAP Ariba, Ivalua, Jaggaer, GEP). The deal is in a formal, consultant-led RFP—Zycus is certainly being compared to at least 2-3 other procurement platforms, but the field is opaque. Without knowing the competitors, Zycus cannot tailor its positioning (e.g. emphasize Merlin AI vs Coupa's weaker AI, or emphasize SAP integration depth vs Ariba's ERP lock-in), cannot anticipate rival pricing or feature gaps, and cannot coach a champion to counter competitor strengths. The 65-day silence may indicate that a competitor is winning or that the buyer is favoring an incumbent/do-nothing path. The immediate move is to ask EY or Claudio directly: who else is in the running, what is the shortlist, and how is Zycus positioned relative to the other vendors. If they will not share, Zycus should infer from the buyer's stated priorities (SAP integration, modularity, AI, renewals automation) which rivals are the likely fit (Coupa, Ivalua, possibly SAP Ariba if they want ERP-native, or an incumbent like Oracle or a legacy on-premise system being replaced).

identify_pain.narrative: The core pain was articulated clearly on the 22 April call by speaker 3 (buyer side): 'Lack of sustained value: initial savings drop off after six months due to weak contract renewal processes' (Avoma note, 22 April). This is an operational and financial pain: procurement achieves initial cost reductions (likely through better sourcing or negotiation) but loses those gains within six months because contract renewals are poorly managed—suppliers revert to higher prices, or renewal terms are not renegotiated. The pain owner is procurement (Claudio Díaz, Jefe de Proyectos ERP, and the broader procurement function managing SAP Solped and supplier follow-up per the workflow discussion). The buyer workflow note confirms manual, fragmented processes: 'Procurement manually converts Solped into purchase orders, manages catalogs, negotiations, and supplier follow-up' (Avoma note, 22 April), and 'Contract management handled in separate workflows with legal, tax, and compliance handoffs' (Avoma note, 22 April). These manual, siloed workflows explain why renewals are weak—no systematic renewal tracking or proactive renegotiation. The pain is confirmed and well-articulated, but not quantified (no dollar figure on lost savings). Zycus iContract and iSource modules directly address this pain (automated contract lifecycle, renewal alerts, sourcing event automation), and the demo positioned them as the solution (Avoma key takeaway: 'Flexible modular procurement platform can be adopted module-by-module or as full ERP replacement', speaker 2, 22 April). The wedge is clear: Zycus sustains savings through disciplined, automated renewal management where On Net Fibra's current manual process fails.

EY (Clementina Villalba, Oscar Otero, Carolina Herrera, Esteban Prieto, Pablo Arnuncio) is running the evaluation on behalf of On Net Fibra, which suggests a consulting-led procurement transformation or ERP selection. The buyer's internal workflow was discussed: 'Purchase requests initiated via the relevant stakeholder after pre-comité and comité approvals' (Avoma note, 22 April), indicating a formal approval chain (pre-committee, full committee) before any purchase. Next Step entries trace the process: clarifications due 14 April, responses 16 April, RFP due 27 April, RFI submitted 28 April. However, no post-RFI timeline was shared—no shortlist date, no finalist demo window, no decision target. Zycus does not know: (a) how many vendors are in the running, (b) when the shortlist will be announced, (c) who the final decision-makers are (the comité membership), or (d) the approval path (does the comité recommend to a CFO/CEO, or does it decide?). The 65-day silence suggests the process has stalled at EY or at the buyer's committee level. The move is to ask EY for the post-RFI timeline, shortlist, and next milestone (finalist demos, pricing round, vendor selection date).

ai_fit_signal.summary: No AIS (AI Score) field populated in Salesforce, so no authoritative AI appetite read from the account-level scoring. From the 22 April demo, Zycus presented Merlin agentic AI ('Native generative-AI agents cover supplier management, contracts, sourcing, eProcurement, invoicing and spend analysis with conversational intake', speaker 3), but the buyer's response was not captured—no explicit enthusiasm, no AI-driven use case requested, no questions on Merlin capability depth. The buyer's pain (savings drop-off due to weak renewals) is solvable with AI (automated renewal tracking + negotiation suggestions), but the buyer has not articulated an AI appetite or AI-first procurement vision. Tier: likely AI Curious (open to AI as a value-add, but not leading with AI as the decision driver) rather than AI Hungry (seeking AI-first transformation). The risk is that if the buyer does not value AI differentiation, Merlin becomes a cost add-on rather than a wedge, and a lower-cost competitor without strong AI may win on price. The move is to convert AI Curious to AI Hungry by tying Merlin explicitly to the renewals pain and quantifying the AI-driven savings lift.

north_star_verdict.headline: 65-day buyer silence following 22 April demo and 28 April RFI submission. EY-led multi-vendor process stalled with no shortlist shared, no replies to three rep follow-ups. Single-threaded, competition unknown, momentum cooling—urgent champion re-engagement needed.

flags: Single-threaded relationship: only one buyer-side contact role (Claudio Díaz, project manager) on a $150k multi-module SaaS deal with a 188-day sales cycle. Multi-threading to the Economic Buyer, senior procurement leader, and finance/CFO is critical to gain control and visibility. Without it, Zycus is dependent on one passive contact and has no direct line to the decision maker.

No champion developed: Claudio Díaz has not demonstrated advocacy, and the buyer has gone quiet for 65 days (no response to follow-ups on 12 May, 26 May, 16 June). A champion with access to the EB and competitive intelligence is the single most important asset to win this deal. The immediate move is to re-engage Claudio and the EY lead, request an intro to senior stakeholders, and ask for a debrief on the RFI and shortlist.

Extract the competitive shortlist and tailor positioning accordingly. The RFI/RFP process guarantees Zycus is being compared to 2-3 rivals, but operating blind is a severe handicap. When re-engaging, ask EY or Claudio: 'Who else are you evaluating, and how do we compare?' ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus showcased Merlin agentic AI on the 22 April demo ('Native generative-AI agents cover supplier management, contracts, sourcing, eProcurement, invoicing and spend analysis with conversational intake', Avoma note 22 April, speaker 3), positioning it as a core differentiator. However, no explicit buyer AI appetite or AI-driven business case was captured—the buyer's questions focused on SAP integration, licensing, and POC, not AI capability depth. The AI story was presented but not anchored to a buyer pain or ROI target, so it may have been heard as 'nice to have' rather than 'must have'. If a competitor (e.g. Coupa with weaker AI, or a legacy system with no AI) is winning on price or ERP-native fit, Merlin's value may be under-positioned. The move is to tie Merlin directly to the buyer's articulated pain (renewals automation: 'Merlin proactively alerts on upcoming renewals and suggests renegotiation strategies, sustaining your savings beyond six months') and to quantify the AI ROI (e.g. 'Merlin reduces manual contract review time by 60%, freeing 2 FTEs for strategic sourcing'). Without this anchoring, AI is a feature, not a wedge.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: On Net Fibra's articulated needs align well with Zycus's core strengths, but the fit has not been validated in writing or through a POC, and the 65-day silence suggests the buyer may not be convinced or may have found a better-fit alternative. Key alignment: (1) Pain = savings drop-off due to weak renewals; Zycus iContract + sourcing automation directly address this. (2) SAP integration is critical (they run SAP Solped); Zycus emphasized SAP integration on the demo ('Integration with SAP is handled by Zycus/Eway', 22 April). (3) Modularity is valued ('Flexible modular procurement platform can be adopted module-by-module', 22 April); Zycus offers phased adoption. (4) AI capability surfaced ('Native generative-AI agents cover supplier management, contracts, sourcing, eProcurement, invoicing and spend analysis', 22 April); Merlin is a differentiator IF the buyer values AI (no explicit AI appetite signal captured). Exposure: (1) No quantified ROI case—buyer articulated pain but not dollar savings target; without a business case, executive buy-in is at risk. (2) No POC executed—buyer asked about POC readiness, Zycus offered it, but no POC was scoped or scheduled; a competitor running a live POC has an advantage. (3) Pricing sensitivity unknown—$150k is the internal Zycus estimate, but no buyer budget discussion occurred; they may be price-constrained (Chile/LATAM market, mid-size telco). (4) 65-day silence suggests misalignment somewhere (capability gap, pricing, competing priority, or simply deprioritized evaluation).

hard.ais_why:
• The process is EY-led. The next-step notes read "EY led demo today, working on RFP pricing" (4/22/26) and "submitted RFI - waiting on next steps" (4/28/26). The buyer has not engaged directly on AI. Products include ML iAnalyze, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI and ANA - a broad AI surface, but it is Zycus scoping rather than a buyer ask.
• AI importance is rated 'Neutral' and both the description and opportunity info fields are empty. No buyer-stated mention of Merlin, Agentic, GenAI, intake or ANA appears anywhere on the record. The telecom context, an EY-led evaluation and an RFP-driven process look like a cost and feature checklist exercise rather than an AI-led transformation.
• Avoma was fully exhausted - zero calls at opportunity, account or onnetfibra.com domain level. That is consistent with an EY-managed offline RFP. With Neutral AI importance and zero buyer voice on AI, the Curious score is generous but defensible while the EY process plays out.

A 65-day silence after a formal RFI submission is not normal long-cycle patience—it signals deprioritization, a competitor winning, or an internal stall. Acting now prevents the deal from going fully dark.

value.flag: Map the Economic Buyer and secure a commercial conversation in the next 30 days. Claudio Díaz is a project manager, not the budget owner; the $150k decision will be made by a CFO, VP Finance, or General Manager. Ask Claudio (or EY) directly: 'Who controls the procurement software budget, and when can we present the ROI case to them?' Without EB access, Zycus cannot validate the budget, cannot tailor pricing, and cannot advance past RFI evaluation to commercial negotiation. If the EB is inaccessible, the deal is not real—disqualify or downgrade forecast.

value.flag: Extract the competitive shortlist and tailor positioning accordingly. The RFI/RFP process guarantees Zycus is being compared to 2-3 rivals, but operating blind is a severe handicap. When re-engaging, ask EY or Claudio: 'Who else are you evaluating, and how do we compare?' If they won't share, infer from the buyer's priorities (SAP integration depth, renewals automation, AI, modularity) which rivals are likely in the running (Coupa, Ivalua, SAP Ariba, possibly an incumbent on-premise system), and proactively position Zycus's wedge: Merlin AI for conversational procurement (vs Coupa's weaker AI), iContract renewals automation (vs weak renewal tracking in Ariba/Ivalua), and modular flexibility (vs full-ERP lock-in). Without competitive intelligence, Zycus is reacting, not leading.
